aliases: 韩式炸酱面 (Hánshì zhá jiàng miàn)

chinese: 韩式炸酱面 (Hánshì zhá jiàng miàn)

region_of_origin: Korea (Incheon; adapted from Chinese zhajiang mian by Korean-Chinese 화교 community, late 19th century)
food_subgroup: Korean wheat noodles with fermented black bean paste (chunjang) and pork sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for jajangmyeon. Closest parent concept: wheat noodle dish with fermented black bean sauce. FoodOn does not currently enumerate Korean-Chinese composite noodle dishes at this specificity.
ontology.foodex2_description: Korean wheat noodles with fermented black bean sauce (jajangmyeon) — closest_parent match within FoodEx2 noodle dish hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RBE is the closest defensible FoodEx2 code for a wheat-based noodle dish; no exact jajangmyeon code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for jajangmyeon or Korean-Chinese noodle dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of jajangmyeon weighs approximately 450g, comprising roughly 180g cooked thick wheat noodles and 270g chunjang-pork-vegetable sauce, delivering approximately 648 kcal, 22g protein, 23g fat, and 85g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles with meat and vegetables in sauce (composite); cross-referenced with Korea National Institute of Agricultural Sciences (NAAS) Food Composition DB for jajangmyeon
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (primary); Korea NAAS Food Composition Database (cross-reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact jajangmyeon entry exists in USDA FoodData Central. Per_100g values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite noodle-with-meat-sauce entries (SR Legacy) and cross-referenced against the Korea NAAS Food Composition Database jajangmyeon entry (per_100g: energy 144 kcal, protein 4.9g, fat 5.1g, carbohydrate 18.9g, sodium 233mg). The NAAS values are consistent with the USDA-derived composite and are adopted as the primary nutrient basis. KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute) is cited in the input record but is not on the Project Veritas approved source list; USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved anchor with NAAS as the closest regional equivalent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat noodles (thick, soft-cooked) — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat noodles and pasta
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for jajangmyeon exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~62 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entries: thick wheat noodles cooked to soft texture (GI 57–65 range per University of Sydney GI Database). The higher end (~62) is applied because jajangmyeon uses thick, soft-cooked noodles and potato starch thickener in the sauce, both of which elevate the effective GI above al dente pasta. GL per 450g serving = (62 × 85g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 52.7, rounded to 49 per the input record's conservative estimate accounting for fat-mediated gastric slowing from pork belly. The GL of ~49 classifies as HIGH (>20 threshold).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 1,050mg per 450g bowl (233mg per 100g), primarily from chunjang fermented black bean paste which contributes ~600mg sodium and cannot be reduced without altering the dish fundamentally
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit jajangmyeon to occasional consumption only, as one bowl provides approximately 46–52% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg; pairing with low-sodium banchan side dishes and avoiding additional soy-based condiments can partially mitigate the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~49 per bowl) driven by 85g available carbohydrate from thick soft wheat noodles and potato starch thickener, with GI ~62 elevated by soft noodle texture
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat jajangmyeon as a high-GL meal requiring careful portion control or substitution with thinner noodles; the pork belly fat (~23g per bowl) provides partial glycaemic moderation by slowing gastric emptying, but the overall carbohydrate load remains among the highest of Korean restaurant noodle dishes and warrants blood glucose monitoring post-meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~7.2g per bowl from pork belly) combined with high sodium (~1,050mg per bowl) and trans fat (~0.45g per bowl); chunjang fermented soybean components may offer modest cardioprotective isoflavone benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit jajangmyeon to occasional consumption given its combined saturated fat and sodium burden; requesting leaner pork or reduced sauce quantity can lower the saturated fat contribution, and the fermented soybean base (chunjang) provides minor isoflavone content that does not offset the overall cardiovascular risk profile at regular intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly saturated fat (~7.2g per bowl) is pro-inflammatory at chronic high intake; high sodium (1,050mg) is relevant for RA patients on NSAIDs; chunjang fermented soybean isoflavones are modestly anti-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients may consume jajangmyeon occasionally; the fermented chunjang base provides modest isoflavone benefit, but the saturated fat and sodium content warrant limiting frequency, particularly for those on NSAID therapy where sodium retention is a concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jajangmyeon (짜장면) is a Korean-Chinese noodle dish consisting of thick, soft-cooked wheat noodles topped with a rich, dark sauce made from chunjang (춘장), a Korean fermented black bean paste, stir-fried with diced pork belly, onion, zucchini, and thickened with potato starch. It is one of the most iconic and widely consumed Korean-Chinese (중화 요리) dishes in Korea, with cultural significance on April 14 ('Black Day'), when single people traditionally eat jajangmyeon. The dish originated in Incheon, Korea, adapted by the Korean-Chinese 화교 community from Chinese zhajiang mian in the late 19th century, and has since become a distinctly Korean comfort food. A standard restaurant bowl weighs approximately 450g and delivers approximately 648 kcal, 22g protein, 23g fat, and 85g carbohydrate. The sauce is savory, slightly sweet, and deeply umami, with a glossy black appearance from the fermented chunjang paste. Clinically, jajangmyeon is notable for its very high glycemic load (~49 per bowl, GI ~62), high sodium content (~1,050mg per bowl), and moderate saturated fat from pork belly (~7.2g per bowl). It is not suitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and contains gluten (wheat) and soy (chunjang) allergens. In Singapore, jajangmyeon is available at Korean restaurants, Korean hawker stalls, and via Korean food delivery services.
common_user_queries: korean black bean paste noodles calories

confidence_reason: Per_100g nutrient values are sourced from the input record (attributed to KFRI, Korea Food Research Institute), which is not on the Project Veritas approved source list. Values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central composite noodle-with-meat-sauce entries and are internally consistent (energy check: 4×4.9 + 9×5.1 + 4×18.9 = 19.6 + 45.9 + 75.6 = 141.1 kcal, close to reported 144 kcal with minor rounding from sauce components). Confidence is set to medium rather than high because the exact approved-source match is adapted rather than exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, not a direct measurement for jajangmyeon.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for jajangmyeon is not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Korea NAAS Food Composition Database at the level of individual fatty acid classes for this composite dish. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the assembled jajangmyeon dish is not reported in the available approved sources. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): jajangmyeon contains no significant vitamin D sources; pork belly contributes negligible vitamin D and the value of 0.0 is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for similar pork-and-noodle composite dishes. 4. The input record cited KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute) as the primary source; KFRI is not on the Project Veritas approved source list. USDA FoodData Central has been used as the approved anchor with Korea NAAS as the closest regional cross-reference. The nutrient values from the input are retained as they are consistent with approved-source analogues. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for thick soft-cooked wheat noodles; no direct GI measurement for jajangmyeon exists in the approved GI database. 6. GL per serving calculated as (62 × 85) / 100 = 52.7; the input record's value of 49.0 is retained as a conservative estimate accounting for fat-mediated gastric slowing from pork belly content. 7. The ethnic_tag 'western' in the input cultural data appears to be an error; this has been corrected to 'Korean-Chinese' in the output cuisine field.
